Off Calendar Oct-03-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Atty. Edward Johnson, Esq. (via Zoom videoconference) of Affeld England & Johnson LLP (tel: 310-979-8700) for Plaintiffs Museum Plaza LLC and Waterview Development LLC.
Atty. Erik Weiss, Esq. of Murphy, Pearson, Bradley & Feeney, PC (tel: 415-788-1900) for Defendant Berding & Weil, LLP.

Hearing Re: CROSS DEFENDANT KATALYST DEVELOPMENT's MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION.

Matter argued and submitted. Upon conclusion, the Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Cross-Defendant Katalyst Development, LLC's motion for summary adjudication is denied. Katalyst moves for summary adjudication only as to Cross-Complainant Berding & Weil LLP's eleventh cause of action for promissory fraud against Katalyst.

This case arises out of a dispute regarding attorney compensation and representation. Walid Mando controls Waterview Development LLC, Katalyst Development LLC, and Museum Plaza LLC. These companies were used by Mando to develop properties. Berding & Weil LLP began representing Waterview in August of 2017. Katalyst agreed to pay by the hour for all time spent on Waterview's legal matters on October 31, 2019. Berding alleges that Katalyst's principal did not intend to keep that promise at the time he made it.

On summary judgment, a moving party must present evidence. (Code Civ. Proc., section 437c, subd. (b)(1).) "Initially, the moving party bears a burden of production to make a prima facie showing of the nonexistence of any genuine issue of material fact. If he carries his burden of production, he causes a shift: the opposing party is then subjected to a burden of production of his own to make a prima facie showing of the existence of a genuine issue of material fact." (Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 845.)

The elements of promissory fraud are " '(1) a promise made regarding a material fact without any intention of performing it; (2) the existence of the intent not to perform at the time the promise was made; (3) intent to deceive or induce the promisee to enter into a transaction; (4) reasonable reliance by the promisee; (5) nonperformance by the party making the promise; and (6) resulting damage to the promise[e].' " (Rossberg v. Bank of America, N.A. (2013) 219 Cal.App.4th 1481, 1498 quoting Behnke v. State Farm General Ins. Co. (2011) 196 Cal.App.4th 1443, 1453.)

The court denies Katalyst's summary adjudication motion for the same reasons as it denied Waterview's motion on the same issue. Without direct evidence of intent, the factfinder will make a decision about intent based on circumstantial evidence, including nonperformance and other factors. Here, there is sufficient circumstantial evidence of a protracted payment dispute from a jury could infer that Katalyst's principal did not intend to perform fully at the time he made the promise to pay.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the court's ruling.

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar Oct-03-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Atty. Edward Johnson, Esq. (via Zoom videoconference) of Affeld England & Johnson LLP (tel: 310-979-8700) for Plaintiffs Museum Plaza LLC and Waterview Development LLC.
Atty. Erik Weiss, Esq. of Murphy, Pearson, Bradley & Feeney, PC (tel: 415-788-1900) for Defendant Berding & Weil, LLP.

Hearing Re: CROSS DEFENDANT WATERVIEW DEVELOPMENT LLC's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT.

Matter argued and submitted. Upon conclusion, the Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Cross-Defendant Waterview Development, LLC's motion for summary adjudication is denied. Waterview moves for summary adjudication only as to Cross-Complainant Berding & Weil LLP's tenth cause of action for promissory fraud against Waterview.

This case arises out of a dispute regarding attorney compensation and representation. Walid Mando controls Waterview Development LLC and Museum Plaza LLC. Both companies were used by Mando to develop properties. Berding & Weil LLP began representing Waterview in August of 2017. The court granted Berding & Weil's motion to withdraw as counsel on July 20, 2021. Waterview filed its original complaint in this current action on December 3, 2021 and the operative Second Amended Complaint on June 5, 2023. Berding & Weil filed a cross complaint on September 25, 2025.

On summary judgment or summary adjudication, a moving party must present evidence. (Code Civ. Proc., section 437c, subd. (b)(1).) "Initially, the moving party bears a burden of production to make a prima facie showing of the nonexistence of any genuine issue of material fact. If he carries his burden of production, he causes a shift: the opposing party is then subjected to a burden of production of his own to make a prima facie showing of the existence of a genuine issue of material fact." (Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 845.)

The elements of promissory fraud are " '(1) a promise made regarding a material fact without any intention of performing it; (2) the existence of the intent not to perform at the time the promise was made; (3) intent to deceive or induce the promisee to enter into a transaction; (4) reasonable reliance by the promisee; (5) nonperformance by the party making the promise; and (6) resulting damage to the promise[e].' " (Rossberg v. Bank of America, N.A. (2013) 219 Cal.App.4th 1481, 1498 quoting Behnke v. State Farm General Ins. Co. (2011) 196 Cal.App.4th 1443, 1453.)

Here, the moving party is not able to show a nonexistence of a genuine material fact. The intention of Waterview's principal Mando as to whether he was actually going to pay for the legal services already provided as well as the services Waterview continued to contract for is not so decisively established in the evidence presented to the court here that summary adjudication is warranted. Waterview essentially asks this court to draw inferences in its favor from the lack of any direct evidence of intent, such as a statement by Mando. But a party may prove promissory fraud through failure to pay and surrounding circumstances. Here, the inferences a jury may draw from the parties' protracted disputes over fee payments can support a finding that Waterview did not intend to pay.

The court also rejects Waterview's economic loss argument. Promissory fraud is a recognized exception to the economic loss rule.

Berding asks the court to strike Waterview's motion for summary adjudication because Waterview was not in good standing when it was filed. Because Waterview has received a revivor, the court does not strike the MSA and considers the reply brief attached as Exhibit A to its notice of status filed on September 22, 2025.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the court's ruling.
